text.title: AI LLM Workload Architecture Core Rules
text.summary: Four testable rules every application of AI LLM Workload Architecture enforces. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: The AI LLM Workload Architecture ground-truth set is versioned and frozen before each comparison run. Adding examples mid-comparison invalidates the comparison.

rationale.source: ML reproducibility / Pineau et al., Reproducibility Checklist 2020

rule.rationale: Mutable eval sets produce non-comparable metrics. Score 0.82 → 0.85 is meaningless if the denominator changed.

rule.statement: Each eval reports n, mean, and confidence interval — not a single number.

rationale.source: statistics / Wasserstein & Lazar, ASA Statement on p-values (2016)

rule.rationale: Naked mean ignores sampling noise. 0.82 ± 0.08 over n=30 is a different story than 0.82 ± 0.01 over n=1000.

rule.statement: Judge-LLM scoring is recalibrated against ≥20 human-labeled examples per quarter. Drift in the judge invalidates downstream metrics.

rationale.source: LLM-as-judge research / Zheng et al., MT-Bench (2023)

rule.rationale: Judge models change. Without recalibration the eval becomes a measurement of the judge, not the target.

rule.statement: Per-example cost is logged. Eval that costs >2× the production daily traffic is downsampled or moved to nightly.
rule.rationale: Unbounded eval budgets break the cost case and reduce eval frequency, which defeats the purpose.

example.title: Versioned eval report
example.code: set: golden-v3-frozen-2026-05-15 (n=247) model: claude-opus-4.6 prompt: jtbd-synthesis@a3f1c (git SHA) result: pass-rate 0.83 ± 0.04 (95% CI)
example.note: Every element of the comparison is pinned. Reproducible.

example.title: Naked eval result
example.note: Unknown set, unknown model, no CI. Useless for regression detection.
